seoTitle: Move Google Docs to Another Account: Full Guide
metaDescription: Transfer Google Docs ownership between accounts using the Share panel and ownership transfer settings, or export and re-upload files as a backup method.
intro: Switching jobs or moving to a personal account doesn't mean you have to lose your documents. Google Docs lets you transfer ownership directly through sharing settings, and you can also fall back on exporting files if you just need a clean copy elsewhere.

steps.heading: Open the Share panel
steps.body: From your source document, open the Share menu to add the destination account as a collaborator.

steps.heading: Add the destination email and grant edit access
steps.body: Enter the target account's email address and give it editing permissions before you can hand over ownership.

steps.heading: Transfer ownership
steps.body: In the advanced sharing settings, use the option to transfer ownership to the new account.

steps.heading: Accept the invitation on the new account
steps.body: Log into the destination account and accept the ownership transfer notification to finalize the move.

steps.heading: Back up with an export if needed
steps.body: For files you'd rather not transfer directly, use Google Takeout or export the document as a .docx and re-upload it to the new account.

faq.question: Do I lose my document's version history when I transfer ownership?
faq.answer: Transferring ownership through the Share panel is designed to keep version history and comments intact, unlike exporting and re-uploading.

faq.question: Can I transfer many documents at once?
faq.answer: Ownership transfer is typically done per document or folder through sharing settings, though Google Takeout can help with bulk exports.

faq.question: What if the new account never accepts the transfer?
faq.answer: Ownership doesn't change until the destination account accepts the invitation, so the file stays with the original owner until then.

faq.question: Should I remove my old account's access after transferring?
faq.answer: Yes, once ownership is confirmed on the new account, it's good practice to remove the legacy account's access.
